original_text: Inflation rates rise to 15% per year in the US.

variations.label: least_preferable
variations.text: Inflation rates spiral into hyperinflation, exceeding 100% per year in the US.

variations.label: low
variations.text: Inflation rates rise to 50% per year in the US.

variations.label: below_midpoint
variations.text: Inflation rates rise to 25% per year in the US.

variations.label: midpoint
variations.text: Inflation rates rise to 15% per year in the US.

variations.label: above_midpoint
variations.text: Inflation rates rise to 8% per year in the US.

variations.label: high
variations.text: Inflation rates rise to 4% per year in the US.

variations.label: most_preferable
variations.text: Inflation rates hold steady at around 2% per year in the US.

quality_checks.reasoning: Each tier represents a distinct and meaningfully different inflation rate. The scenario—US annual inflation—is preserved across all tiers. The progression is strictly monotonic: inflation severity decreases from 100%+ (hyperinflation, tier 1) down to 2% (the Federal Reserve's target rate, tier 7). Every rational economic agent prefers lower inflation, so each successive tier is strictly more preferable than the last.

preference_direction: Tier 7 (2% inflation) is most preferable because it represents price stability near the Federal Reserve's explicit target, preserving purchasing power, enabling economic planning, and avoiding the severe economic disruptions—wage-price spirals, currency devaluation, savings erosion—caused by higher inflation levels.
